title: Low Rank Structure of the Reduced Transition Matrix
authors: Cathy Li, Bruno Bertini, Katja Klobas, Tianci Zhou
abstract: The influence-matrix formalism provides an alternative route to the classical simulation of quantum dynamics. Because influence matrices retain information only about the effective bath seen by local observables, they are expected to be easier to simulate than the full wavefunction. Recent work, however, has shown that they carry strong temporal correlations even in maximally chaotic systems, making them difficult to represent efficiently. Here we show that the reduced transition matrix, a suitable combination of influence matrices that directly determines local expectation values, can nevertheless be efficiently approximated. We first show that the truncation error is controlled by its singular-value spectrum, which naturally motivates a low-rank approximation. We then prove that, for chaotic dual-unitary circuits, the associated entropy grows at most logarithmically in time. Our conclusions follow from exact results for random dual-unitary circuits and are further supported by numerical results for fixed instances of both dual-unitary and random circuits.
summary: The paper shows that while influence matrices in chaotic quantum circuits exhibit high entanglement, the related reduced transition matrix (RTM) has a low-rank structure with only logarithmic entropy growth. This discovery enables an efficient joint compression method for simulating local expectation values in complex quantum dynamics.
main_problem: The difficulty of classically simulating quantum many-body dynamics due to the volume-law entanglement growth in influence matrices, even in chaotic systems.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the reduced transition matrix (RTM) can be efficiently approximated via low-rank approximation because its von Neumann entropy grows at most logarithmically with time, despite the high entanglement of its constituent influence matrices.
method: The study uses the influence-matrix formalism, the folding algorithm, and a joint compression procedure. Analytical proofs involve the entanglement membrane approach and the Schmidt-Mirsky theorem, while numerical validation uses random and dual-unitary circuits.
model_or_system: The study focuses on non-equilibrium quantum many-body dynamics, specifically using brickwork quantum circuits, including chaotic dual-unitary circuits and Haar-random unitary circuits.
key_observables: Local expectation values (one-point functions), von Neumann entropy of the RTM, singular-value spectrum, and the Schatten 1-norm of the truncation error.
important_parameters: Entangling power (p), time (t), dimension of the local Hilbert space (d), and the critical entangling power (pc).
main_assumptions: The validity of the scaling of amplitudes away from the critical point is treated as a conjecture supported by the membrane approach; the paper also assumes the availability of influence matrices for the compression procedure.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of one-point functions and the RTM construction; Figure 2 compares standard MPS compression with the proposed joint compression; Figure 4 illustrates the probability distribution and entropy growth in dual-unitary and random circuits; Supplemental figures visualize domain wall and magnon configurations.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of influence matrix entanglement, proposes the RTM low-rank approximation, provides error control proofs, establishes logarithmic entropy bounds for dual-unitary circuits, and validates these findings with numerical simulations of random and non-random circuits.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it provides a new pathway for the efficient classical simulation of local observables in highly chaotic systems where standard wavefunction-based methods fail due to entanglement growth.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The influence matrices. (a) Schematic representation of a one-point function, where the forward and backward time sheets are shown in red and blue, respectively. (b) Folded representation of the one-point function, the left ⟨L| and right |R⟩influence matrices. (c) Tensor-network representation of the influence matrices. Partial contraction of the lower legs defines the reduced transition matrix.

FIG. 2. The standard MPS compression and joint compres- sion based on the reduced transition matrix. The diamond P represents the projection required to truncate the singu- lar value spectrum. (a) The standard truncation procedure applied to each link of an L2 normalized MPS. (b) The equiva- lent computation through the influence matrices and the joint truncation procedure of the reduced transition matrix.

FIG. 3. Diagrammatic representation of the circuit geometry and the reduced transition matrix construction. We consider an initial state of the form |Ψ0⟩= QL i=1 |ψ0⟩, where |ψ0⟩is represented by a red circle with legs pointing up and, analogously, ⟨ψ0| by a blue circle with legs pointing down. Red squares denote local gates and blue squares their Hermitian conjugates. (a) State |Ψt⟩after t layers of a brickwork circuit. (b) Folded circuit representation of a one-point function in terms of the influence matrices ⟨L| and |R⟩. Gates outside the light cones can be removed by unitarity; gates in the green region can be removed by dual unitarity without affecting the singular spectrum. (c) Local...

FIG. 4. (a) Probability distribution {pk} versus k for a fixed dual-unitary circuit with p = 0.625 and t = 15. The distribution is almost flat for k ≥1, especially with larger t0. The decay at each fixed k is well described by an exponential in t0. (b) Exact numerical evaluation of maxt0 S(ρt0) together with the upper and lower bounds from Eq. (11) for a non-random dual-unitary circuit with p = 0.625. (c) Same comparison for a generic unitary circuit obtained from a Haar-random gate fixed in space and time.
